reasoning_summary: For live run 20260421_182631_bf16_gemm_v1_1f02b147 at 25.063408 ms, rolling __launch_bounds__ back to 2 recovered most of the bad barrier and short-scoreboard tax from 49dfa799: barrier fell from 9.73% to 6.35% and short-scoreboard fell from 7.32% to 1.86%. The trade is that registers climbed back to 202, occupancy_limit_registers fell to 2, active warps fell to 16.60%, and long_scoreboard plus mio_throttle rose to 5.45% and 3.98%. That shifts the dominant bottleneck from synchronization back to latency hiding and async overlap. I do not rank the old register-interleave trim first because state/search_closed.jsonl already records that exact fingerprint once as BUILD_FAIL and once as a 31.239679 ms PASS_LOSS. Instead I rank a 3-stage Pg2s pipeline first because it attacks the now-dominant long_scoreboard and mio_throttle pair without replaying a known-bad register-reuse fingerprint. I keep the final-drain wait/sync split second as the smallest remaining PTX barrier cleanup still open, and I leave the writer-safe 256x128 reopen third so the user-requested 256x128/64x64 family stays alive only after the current PTX surface is squeezed further.
family_audit: accept: prioritize async-copy and Pg2s-stage families on the current PTX surface because long_scoreboard and mio_throttle are now the clearest remaining losses after barrier recovery.
defer: do not replay the old PTX register-interleave trim fingerprint this round; the exact action already has one BUILD_FAIL and one 31.239679 ms PASS_LOSS recorded in state/search_closed.jsonl.
defer_but_keep_open: keep one writer-safe 256x128 reopen direction in rank 3 to honor the persistent 256x128 / 64x64 guidance, but only after the local PTX latency-hiding families are tested.

directions.name: Deepen The Active PTX Hot-Band To A 3-Stage Pg2s Pipeline
directions.hypothesis: After restoring launch_bounds to 2, the PTX anchor regained low barrier and low short-scoreboard, but the remaining gap is now long_scoreboard plus mio_throttle at only 16.60% active warps. A 3-stage Pg2s ring should hide more global-to-shared latency by keeping one extra refill in flight, so the kernel can recover tensor activity without needing the failed register-interleave rewrite.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Global-to-shared latency hiding at low occupancy: long_scoreboard 5.45% and mio_throttle 3.98% indicate cp.async completion is still arriving too late for the current two-stage PTX pipeline.
directions.risk: Moderate-high. This touches the PTX prolog, the steady-state refill order, and the drain sequencing together, and it can easily grow shared memory or destabilize wait_group ordering if done sloppily.
ranking_notes: The current run already cleaned up barrier, so the remaining visible gap is the latency-hiding pair long_scoreboard plus mio_throttle.
This follows the Async Copy, Pg2s, and Stage guidance directly without replaying the exact register-interleave fingerprint that already failed.
It keeps the active PTX dispatch and only deepens overlap; if it loses, the result will still teach whether the current surface is pipeline-limited or occupancy-limited.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the edit forces shared memory materially above the current PTX budget or if wait_group sequencing requires a broader launch-family rewrite.
expected_local_changes: Expand the PTX a_shared and b_shared ring from two stages to three.
Retune the PTX prolog and steady-state wait_group ordering to keep an extra refill in flight.
Leave the active PTX dispatch and grouped_rows constant unchanged in the first pipeline-depth probe.

directions.name: Split The Final PTX Wait/Sync Drain Out Of The Steady-State Loop
directions.hypothesis: Even after the launch-bounds rollback, the PTX loop still pays cp_async_wait_group_0 plus __syncthreads whenever next_tile_idx < FixedKTiles, including the penultimate tile where future_tile_idx is already out of range and no refill follows. Pulling that no-refill drain into a tiny epilogue should shave the remaining barrier seam without changing the current register footprint or stage depth.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Residual barrier tax from the final no-refill handoff inside the PTX steady-state loop.
directions.risk: Moderate. The change is local and keeps the same PTX dispatch, but it modifies a performance-sensitive loop boundary and can break correctness if the final drain is mistimed.
ranking_notes: This remains the smallest open PTX-only cleanup on the current accepted base surface.
It is now rank 2 instead of rank 1 because barrier is no longer the dominant loss; long_scoreboard and mio_throttle are larger remaining problems.
If the 3-stage pipeline is too risky or blocked, this is the next cheapest way to probe further synchronization cleanup.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if splitting the drain requires another shared-memory stage, wider launch changes, or extra steady-state predicates beyond a tiny epilogue case.
expected_local_changes: Split the no-refill drain from the current PTX steady-state wait/sync block.
Keep stage count at 2 and leave the current PTX launch surface unchanged.
Do not touch grouped_rows or the 64x64 row-pair accumulator path in the first pass.

directions.name: Reopen 256x128 64x64-Warp Hot-Band Tiling On The Dominant Surface
directions.hypothesis: The compact PTX 128x128 path is now back near its accepted baseline, but it is still almost 0.9 ms off the best known custom result and still capped at 16.60% active warps. The source already carries a 256x128 hot-band surface, and the persistent human guidance still asks for 256x128 block tiling with 64x64 warp tiling plus Right-Left-Right-Left reuse. Reopening that family is the next structural branch once the local PTX surface has been deconfounded.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Hot-band tiling and warp-level reuse ceiling: the 128x128 PTX surface may simply have run out of occupancy and locality headroom.
directions.risk: High. This is a broader geometry reopen with known correctness and performance history, and it touches writer ownership, export layout, and launch routing together.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
ranking_notes: This keeps the explicit 256x128 / 64x64 human guidance alive without pretending it is the first thing to do on a surface that just recovered locally.
It is still the highest-ceiling structural branch left if the local PTX latency-hiding directions plateau again.
Rank 3 is deliberate: the current PTX path is close enough to its accepted baseline that broader tiling work should wait until the local families are squeezed.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the reopen requires a broad writer-ownership replay or reintroduces previously failed export layouts instead of a writer-safe 256x128 path.
expected_local_changes: Reopen the fixed hot-band launch routing for the 256x128 dominant surface.
Keep writer ownership explicit and export-safe.
Preserve the 64x64 warp-tile reuse pattern rather than reviving the broken half-panel writer split.

notes: Round 4 treats 1f02b147 as the new accepted local PTX anchor. The next directions should exploit that recovery instead of replaying the exact register-interleave fingerprint that already failed and regressed.
